concept: Resource allocation under constraints — selecting a subset of items whose values exactly meet a demand, with each item usable once
math_domain: Number and Operations — Combinatorics / Subset-Sum Reasoning

misconceptions.label: Send everything immediately
misconceptions.description: Learner selects all available trucks without checking whether the combined capacity matches the demand. Treats dispatch as urgency fulfillment rather than a constraint-matching problem.
misconceptions.likely_cause: Intuitive 'do more = help more' reasoning transfers from everyday experience. In the real world, sending more resources is usually safer. The constraint that exact match is required is a mathematical rule that contradicts natural intuition.
misconceptions.how_it_appears_in_play: All trucks selected within 2–3 seconds of seeing the incident. No scanning pause before first selection. Pattern persists even when total capacity far exceeds demand. Player expresses surprise when over-dispatch fails.
misconceptions.detection_signal: All available trucks selected within 3000ms of incident appearing. Total selected capacity exceeds demand by ≥ 50%.
misconceptions.best_feedback_response: Check the fire size first — sending more trucks than needed wastes resources for the next call. Match the number on the fire.
misconceptions.best_clean_replay_task: One-truck yard. Demand = exactly that truck's capacity. Player can only send one option. Repeat with two trucks, only one of which matches. Forces comparison before selection.
misconceptions.reflection_prompt: Before you sent the trucks, did you add up their numbers? What would you check first next time?

misconceptions.label: Confuses truck count with capacity
misconceptions.description: Learner believes the demand number represents the number of trucks to send, not the sum of their water capacities. Sends exactly N trucks regardless of their individual capacities.
misconceptions.likely_cause: Counting is the more familiar operation. The concept that objects have weighted values that must sum — not just that objects must be counted — is the precise conceptual leap the game is teaching. Without explicit framing, '3 units needed' reads as '3 trucks needed'.
misconceptions.how_it_appears_in_play: Number of selected trucks consistently equals the demand number. Player ignores capacity values entirely. Truck selection appears arbitrary within the count constraint. Works accidentally when trucks have capacity 1.
misconceptions.detection_signal: Number of selected trucks equals demand value on ≥ 3 consecutive orders. Total selected capacity does not match demand in ≥ 2 of those orders.
misconceptions.best_feedback_response: The number on each truck is how much water it carries. The fire needs X units total — add the truck numbers up, not the truck count.
misconceptions.best_clean_replay_task: Two trucks: one with capacity 3, one with capacity 1. Demand = 3. Player must send the single capacity-3 truck, not three capacity-1 trucks (there's only one anyway). Explicit label: 'Water capacity: 3'.
misconceptions.reflection_prompt: What does the number on the truck tell you? How is that different from just counting trucks?

misconceptions.label: Misreads capacity icon as priority or speed
misconceptions.description: Learner fails to distinguish between truck capacity labels and helicopter capacity labels, treating the capacity number as a shared pool or misattributing a helicopter's capacity value to a selected truck (or vice versa). May also interpret capacity numbers as priority, speed, or distance rank rather than water volume. Selects resources by perceived urgency or by confusing which label belongs to which unit type.
misconceptions.likely_cause: When two distinct unit types (trucks and helicopters) share the same visual format for their capacity label, learners cannot reliably anchor a number to its source unit. In strategy and simulation games, numbers on units often represent attack power, speed, or priority, compounding the misread. Icon proximity, overlapping layouts, or similar badge designs increase the risk that a learner reads a helicopter's capacity value while intending to evaluate a truck, or pools both values incorrectly.
misconceptions.how_it_appears_in_play: Player selects a truck-helicopter combination whose combined capacity appears to meet demand but whose individual labels were cross-read (e.g., reads helicopter capacity as the truck's value and vice versa, arriving at a wrong sum). Player may also dispatch a helicopter to an urban fire believing its capacity label applies the same way as a truck's, or ignore one unit type's labels entirely and rely only on the other type's numbers. Alternatively, player consistently selects the highest-numbered units first regardless of demand or terrain, as in the original pattern.
misconceptions.detection_signal: Dispatch combinations where the selected truck(s) and helicopter(s) have swapped capacity assumptions — i.e., actual combined truck capacity does not meet demand but would if helicopter values were substituted in, or wrong-terrain units are selected with correct-looking capacity sums. Also flagged: truck selection order correlates with capacity rank (always high-first or always low-first) across ≥ 4 consecutive orders regardless of demand value.
misconceptions.best_feedback_response: Each unit has its own capacity number — trucks and helicopters have separate labels. Make sure you're reading the number that belongs to the unit you're selecting. The numbers need to add up to the fire size, using only the units you actually dispatch.
misconceptions.best_clean_replay_task: Text-only mode: replace truck and helicopter icons with clearly color-coded plain cards — blue cards labeled 'Truck Water: 3', 'Truck Water: 1' and orange cards labeled 'Heli Water: 2', 'Heli Water: 4'. Demand card labeled 'Fire needs: 4'. Player matches values using only one color group at a time, then mixed, to practice anchoring label to unit type before reintroducing icons.
misconceptions.reflection_prompt: When you looked at the trucks and helicopters together, could you tell which capacity number belonged to which unit? How did you decide which numbers to add up?

misconceptions.label: Correct strategy, arithmetic error in sum
misconceptions.description: Learner uses the right approach — adds truck capacities to match demand — but makes a calculation error in the final sum. Understands subset-sum reasoning but has an arithmetic accuracy gap.
misconceptions.likely_cause: Mental addition of 2–3 numbers under time pressure is genuinely difficult. The learner is doing the right math but the cognitive load of simultaneous selection and addition exceeds arithmetic fluency. More common at L3+ where truck counts and demand values grow.
misconceptions.how_it_appears_in_play: Selected capacity is off by exactly 1–3 (not far off). Player's selection pattern shows deliberate comparison behavior (pauses, scans options). Strategy is visible even in failed attempts. Close-miss rate is high.
misconceptions.detection_signal: Selected total capacity differs from demand by 1–3 units. Selection time > 3000ms (deliberate). Pattern appears on ≥ 2 consecutive orders without impulsive-tap signals.
misconceptions.best_feedback_response: You were close — your trucks add up to X but the fire needs Y. Count the numbers again.
misconceptions.best_clean_replay_task: Same demand, same truck set, repeat immediately with running capacity sum displayed as trucks are selected. Player sees '3 + 2 = 5' building as they tap.
misconceptions.reflection_prompt: How close was your total to what the fire needed? Where do you think the counting went wrong?

misconceptions.label: Tries to reuse a dispatched truck
misconceptions.description: Learner ignores terrain restrictions and dispatches a fire truck to a forest fire or a helicopter to an urban fire, believing any resource with sufficient capacity can respond to any fire. Additionally, learner may not fully grasp the cooldown mechanic and treat resources on cooldown as immediately available.
misconceptions.likely_cause: In most resource contexts, capacity is the only relevant constraint for matching supply to demand. The terrain-type restriction is a secondary rule that filters the valid subset before capacity is even considered. Without salient visual differentiation between terrain tags and resource icons, learners collapse the two-dimensional constraint (terrain + capacity) into a one-dimensional one (capacity only). Cooldown visibility also creates ambiguity about when a resource is truly ready.
misconceptions.how_it_appears_in_play: Player selects a truck for a forest-tagged fire or a helicopter for an urban-tagged fire and confirms dispatch, receiving a 'wasted dispatch' failure. Player expresses surprise when the resource fails to extinguish the fire despite sufficient capacity. Player may also attempt to select a resource still shown in cooldown state, expecting it to be usable.
misconceptions.detection_signal: Dispatch event where the selected resource type does not match the fire's terrain tag (truck→forest or helicopter→urban). Also: tap event on a resource currently in cooldown state. Either event occurring ≥ 2 times per session.
misconceptions.best_feedback_response: That resource can't reach that fire. Trucks fight urban fires; helicopters fight forest fires. Check the terrain tag before you pick your resources.
misconceptions.best_clean_replay_task: Tutorial: one urban fire (severity 3) and one forest fire (severity 3). Pool shows one truck (capacity 3) and one helicopter (capacity 3), both clearly labeled with terrain icons. Player must dispatch the truck to the urban fire and the helicopter to the forest fire. Attempting the wrong pairing triggers an animated 'blocked route' visual before the player can confirm, then prompts them to reconsider.
misconceptions.reflection_prompt: Before you picked a resource, did you check the fire's terrain tag? What happens when you send a truck to a forest fire instead of a helicopter?

misconceptions.label: Cannot track terrain + capacity + cooldown simultaneously
misconceptions.description: Learner may handle one or two of the three active constraints (terrain compatibility, combined capacity, cooldown availability) correctly in isolation but cannot integrate all three into a single dispatch decision. Optimizes for one or two attributes while ignoring the third, producing dispatches that are partially correct but ultimately invalid or suboptimal.
misconceptions.likely_cause: Three-attribute constraint reasoning requires simultaneously filtering by terrain type, summing capacity values to hit an exact target, and excluding resources on cooldown. Each additional constraint multiplicatively increases working memory demand. This is a cognitive load ceiling issue rather than a conceptual misunderstanding of any individual rule. Most pronounced when a high-severity or time-pressured incident forces rapid decisions across all three dimensions at once.
misconceptions.how_it_appears_in_play: Player selects resources that meet capacity and cooldown constraints but picks the wrong terrain type (e.g., sends a truck to a forest fire). Or selects the correct terrain type and capacity but inadvertently includes a resource still on cooldown, leaving actual dispatch capacity below threshold. Performance degrades specifically when two or more constraints are stressed simultaneously — such as a forest fire with high severity appearing shortly after helicopters entered cooldown. 'Close' selections that satisfy two of the three constraints but fail on the third are the signature pattern.
misconceptions.detection_signal: Terrain-correct selections in ≥ 65% of dispatches AND capacity-correct selections in ≥ 65% of dispatches, but both constraints satisfied together in ≤ 35% of dispatches. Separately, cooldown-violation rate (selecting a resource still in cooldown) spikes to ≥ 40% on rounds immediately following a high-severity incident. Errors cluster on incidents where at least two constraints are non-trivial simultaneously.
misconceptions.best_feedback_response: Check three things in order: first, does the terrain match? Then, do the capacity numbers add up? Finally, are those resources off cooldown? One step at a time.
misconceptions.best_clean_replay_task: Run a single-constraint drill sequence: (1) terrain-only matching for 2 orders with capacity and cooldown hidden, (2) add capacity matching for 2 orders with cooldown hidden, (3) reintroduce cooldown indicators with an explicit step-by-step checklist overlay (terrain → capacity → cooldown) visible in the UI before each dispatch.
misconceptions.reflection_prompt: When you were picking trucks and helicopters, what did you think about first — the type of terrain, how much water you needed, or which vehicles were available? What order would make it easiest to check all three things?
